what_it_does: A thin Node.js CLI wrapper that maps user-facing subcommands and options to an underlying compiled CLI script at ../dist/index-cli.js, prints help, prompts before a destructive rebuild, and exits with the child process's status.
observations: Spawns the real implementation via child_process.spawn using process.execPath and ../dist/index-cli.js.
Subcommand mapping: default -> index-all, --cleanup -> index-cleanup, --session -> index-session, --verify -> verify, --repair -> repair, --rebuild -> rebuild.
--rebuild triggers an interactive 'yes' confirmation before running.
Help text documents --concurrency, -c, --no-summaries, examples, workflow, and references to INDEXING.md and DEPLOYMENT.md.
Uses ESM imports, import.meta.url, fileURLToPath, dirname, and realpathSync to compute __dirname.
Any unrecognized first argument is forwarded to index-all as part of its arguments.
Top-level errors are printed to stderr and the process exits with code 1.
standouts: The CLI contains no indexing logic itself; it is purely a launcher/proxy.
Destructive rebuild is explicitly guarded by a confirmation prompt.
Default branch treats an unknown leading argument as a positional argument for index-all rather than an error.
conventions: ESM module pattern with __filename/__dirname derived from import.meta.url.
Promise wrapper around spawn events (exit and error).
stdio: 'inherit' so the child controls the terminal experience.
concerns: Hardcoded relative path ../dist/index-cli.js assumes a build artifact exists in a specific location.
No validation that --session is followed by an ID before forwarding.
Default fallback may misinterpret flags or typos as arguments for index-all.
Confirmation prompt does not mention any extra arguments passed with --rebuild.
notes: A friendly CLI shim that forwards commands to a built JS CLI and adds a safety prompt for the database-deleting rebuild command.
questions: What exact command set and argument schema does ../dist/index-cli.js expect?
Is the ../dist/index-cli.js path stable across packaging, installation, and deployment?
Should the wrapper validate that --session has a value before spawning?
Are --concurrency and --no-summaries consumed by this wrapper or entirely forwarded?
purpose_vs_actual: The help text says it indexes and manages conversation archives, but this file only launches the script that does that work; as a launcher it matches the stated CLI purpose.
intention_signals: Provide a discoverable, safe command-line frontend for conversation indexing while keeping implementation details in a compiled dist script and protecting dangerous operations with confirmation.
contribution: A stable user-facing entry point that decouples the installed command name from the built implementation path and adds a confirmation gate for the rebuild command.
summary_for_embedding: Node.js CLI wrapper that forwards conversation-indexing subcommands to ../dist/index-cli.js and prompts before rebuilding the database.
intention_for_embedding: To give users a simple, safe command-line frontend for indexing and maintaining conversation archives.

symbols.does: Spawns the underlying dist CLI with a subcommand and arguments.
symbols.description: Wraps child_process.spawn to run ../dist/index-cli.js under the current Node executable, inheriting stdio and resolving or rejecting based on the child process exit code or spawn error.

symbols.does: Prompts the user on stdin and returns true only if the answer is 'yes'.
symbols.description: Uses readline to ask a yes/no question; employed to gate the destructive --rebuild command before it runs.

symbols.does: Prints usage, commands, options, examples, workflow, and documentation references.
symbols.description: Displays the comprehensive help text for the index-conversations CLI, including cost/speed hints and maintenance workflow.

symbols.does: Parses process.argv and dispatches to the appropriate runner or help output.
symbols.description: Reads the first argument to select a subcommand, forwards remaining arguments to the dist CLI, handles help, rebuild confirmation, and top-level error handling with process exit codes.
